title: Baseline retest (no implementation change)
motivation:
Used by the orchestrator integration test (--from-hypothesis path).
Measures the unchanged baseline through the full eval pipeline so the
resulting log entry locks in the first fitness number for phase 8.
hypothesis:
No RTL changes — exercises the worktree -> emit_verilog -> formal ->
cosim -> fpga path on the existing rtl/*.sv to confirm the orchestrator
produces a structured log entry.

changes.description:
Sentinel — no real change. skip_implementation:true below bypasses
the implementation agent, so the file path here is metadata only;
it just has to satisfy the schema's rtl/<file> pattern. core.sv is
always present and untouched.

title: Move DIV/REM off the single-cycle ALU path
motivation:
The baseline ALU places RV32M multiplication and division in the same
combinational EX-stage result mux as common integer operations. A 32-bit
combinational divider is very likely to dominate LUT depth and area on
the Tang Nano 20K, yet CoreMark's timed region is matrix/list/state code
that is multiply-heavy and effectively division-free. Keeping DIV/REM
single-cycle optimizes an uncommon path while lowering Fmax for every
instruction.
hypothesis:
Replace the combinational DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU cases in rtl/alu.sv with a
small iterative RV32M divide/remainder unit and add an EX-stage interlock
only when one of those opcodes is in flight. Leave ADD/SUB/logic/shift,
branches, loads/stores, jumps, and MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU on the current
single-cycle path. The timed CoreMark loop should pay no meaningful IPC
penalty because it does not execute DIV/REM, while the FPGA critical path
should shrink from divider-through-ALU-mux to the existing add/compare/mul
paths. Preserve exact RV32M divide-by-zero and INT_MIN/-1 semantics and
keep the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS substitutions for formal builds.

changes.description:
Remove real combinational DIV/REM hardware from the normal ALU result
mux, retaining single-cycle non-divide ALU and MUL behavior plus the
existing ALTOPS formulas under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS.

Add an iterative signed/unsigned divider/remainder block with start,
busy, done, result, and operation inputs, implementing all RV32M corner
cases before or after the unsigned divide iteration as appropriate.

Detect DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU in EX, start the iterative unit, hold the
ID/EX-to-EX/MEM advancement until done, then write the completed result
into the EX/MEM register with the original instruction metadata.

Add the divide-unit busy/done interlock to the existing stall network so
younger instructions, fetch, and RVFI retirement remain ordered while a
multi-cycle DIV/REM is completing.

Wire the new EX-stage divide stall signal into the top-level hazard
controls without changing the core IO shape or the single-retire RVFI
channel-0 convention.

title: Elastic Fetch Skid Buffer
motivation:
The baseline frontend has no real IF/ID storage: instruction data flows
directly from io_imemData into decode, and hazard_unit globally freezes the
PC on load-use, dmem stalls, and imem stalls. Under the tournament's ~22%
random imem backpressure, every not-ready fetch becomes a visible decode
bubble. Under dmem backpressure, the independent imem port is also idle even
though it could be fetching sequential instructions for after the stall.
CoreMark's IPC is already close to the single-issue limit, so the best
structural win is to recover wasted frontend cycles without widening the
core or lengthening the EX critical path.
hypothesis:
Replace the combinational IF/ID handoff with an elastic 2-entry fetch skid
buffer that decouples instruction fetch from decode. The fetch side advances
the PC and captures sequential instructions whenever io_imemReady is high
and the buffer is not full, even while ID/EX is held by a dmem stall. The
decode side consumes one buffered instruction per non-stalled cycle. Preserve
the baseline zero-latency behavior with an empty-buffer bypass from
io_imemData to decode when the backend is ready and imem is ready, so the
no-stall path and branch target refill latency do not regress. On EX
redirect, flush all queued entries and restart fetch at redirect_target.
This should hide a meaningful fraction of random imem stalls and avoid
wasting fetch bandwidth during backend stalls while keeping the core
single-issue, in-order, and RVFI channel-0-only.

changes.description:
Add a two-entry valid/ready fetch queue with a zero-latency bypass when
the queue is empty. Push fetched {pc,instr} entries on imemReady while
space is available, pop only when decode actually accepts the current
instruction, and flush the queue on reset or redirect.

Stop treating raw io_imemReady as a global pipeline stall. Keep load-use
and dmem interlocks, but let an empty fetch buffer naturally present
valid=0/NOP to ID while the fetch side waits for imemReady.

Wire the frontend consume/full/empty controls between if_stage and the
existing stall/flush network without changing the top-level core IO or
the NRET=2 RVFI port convention.

If needed, extend the IF/ID bundle or add small fetch-buffer typedefs for
queued pc/instruction/valid metadata; no architectural state or RVFI
field widths change.

implementation_notes:
# Elastic Fetch Skid Buffer

## What changed

- Reworked `rtl/if_stage.sv` into a two-entry elastic fetch skid buffer.
  - Empty-buffer bypass preserves the baseline zero-wait path from `io_imemData` to decode.
  - Fetch advances `io_imemAddr` when `io_imemReady` is high and the buffer has room, including while decode is blocked by load-use or dmem interlocks.
  - Decode pops one queued instruction only when the ID stage actually accepts the presented IF/ID item.
  - Reset, flush, and EX redirect clear queued entries; redirect restarts fetch at `redirect_target`.
- Updated `rtl/hazard_unit.sv` so raw `io_imemReady` no longer creates a global pipeline stall. The hazard unit now keeps only load-use and dmem interlocks.
- Updated `rtl/core.sv` wiring to pass `io_imemReady` into IF and remove it from the hazard unit.
- Added `rtl/000_core_pkg.sv`, an early-sorted guarded mirror of `core_pkg.sv`, so the required `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv` command sees typedefs/localparams before alphabetically later RTL consumers. Builds that explicitly read `core_pkg.sv` first see the mirror as a no-op through the shared `CORE_PKG_DEFINED` guard.

## Deviations

- I kept the existing five-stage topology and reused `stall_if` as the IF-side decode backpressure signal rather than adding new top-level internal control names.
- During a dmem stall with a redirecting instruction held in ID/EX, IF still treats the continuously asserted redirect as a squash. This preserves correctness but does not prefetch past that held redirect.

## Concerns

- `000_core_pkg.sv` duplicates declarations from `core_pkg.sv` to satisfy the fixed lint glob order. If `core_pkg.sv` changes later, the mirror must be kept in sync.

## Local status

- Lint: pass with `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`.
- Formal: pass, 105 passed / 0 failed, run from a writable `/tmp/codex-formal-hyp-20260427-002-r1s1` copy because the worktree's `formal/riscv-formal` path is a symlink to a shared checkout outside the writable sandbox. A direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` in this sandbox failed before checks with `Operation not permitted` while clearing the staged core directory.

title: Backward-Branch Taken Predictor
motivation: The current five-stage core resolves all branches and jumps in EX and fetches strictly pc+4 until that resolution, so every taken loop branch in CoreMark pays a two-instruction wrong-path flush. CoreMark's hot loops are dominated by short backward conditional branches whose direction is highly biased toward taken. A small static predictor can remove most loop-back redirect bubbles without adding a learned-state table, RAM, or speculative architectural state.
hypothesis: Add a decode-light static branch predictor in IF that recognizes B-type instructions and predicts backward branches taken when the sign bit of the branch immediate is set. Compute the B immediate in IF, redirect the next PC to pc+imm for predicted-taken backward branches, and carry predicted_taken plus predicted_target through IF/ID and ID/EX. In EX, compare the actual branch result and target against the prediction, redirect only on mispredict, and set rvfi pc_wdata to the actual next PC as today. JAL/JALR remain resolved in EX to keep the predictor slot focused on conditional branches and avoid adding a return-address stack or BTB.

changes.description: Extend IF/ID and ID/EX pipeline bundles with predicted_taken and predicted_target fields so EX can distinguish correct predictions from true mispredicts while preserving RVFI pc_wdata semantics.

Add static backward-branch-taken prediction for B-type instructions: decode the branch immediate from the fetched instruction, select the predicted next PC for the PC register, and emit the prediction metadata with the fetched instruction. Suppress prediction when reset, stall, flush, or an EX redirect is active.

Change redirect generation from always-on taken-branch redirect to mispredict-only redirect. Actual next PC remains pc+4, branch target, or jump target as appropriate, and misalignment traps still suppress redirect and mark the retiring instruction illegal.
Treat EX redirect as a mispredict recovery signal and keep the existing IF/ID and ID/EX flush behavior for wrong-path instructions. No invariant checks or RVFI channel behavior are weakened.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Added `predicted_taken` and `predicted_target` metadata to the IF/ID and ID/EX pipeline bundles.
- Added an IF-stage static predictor for legal B-type conditional branches with a negative immediate. Predicted-taken branches update the fetch PC to `pc + imm` and carry the prediction metadata with the instruction.
- Changed EX redirect generation so conditional branches redirect only when the resolved next PC differs from the predicted next PC. JAL/JALR still redirect from EX, and RVFI `pc_wdata` still reports the resolved architectural next PC.
- Added `rtl/00_core_pkg_first.sv` so the required raw `rtl/*.sv` Verilator lint command sees `core_pkg.sv` definitions before `core.sv`. Normal ordered builds read `core_pkg.sv` first, so this shim is a guard-protected no-op there.

## Deviations

- IF prediction is suppressed for reserved branch funct3 values and for predicted targets with `target[1:0] != 2'b00`. This keeps illegal branch encodings and branch-target misalignment traps on the existing fallthrough/trap path instead of speculatively fetching from a path EX is required not to redirect to.

## Concerns

- The predictor intentionally does not handle JAL/JALR, forward taken branches, or any learned state, matching the hypothesis scope.
- The extra `pc + B-imm` adder is now in IF. That may affect Fmax; the FPGA gate will determine whether the IPC gain outweighs it.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` in the worktree was blocked by sandbox permissions because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared tree outside the writable roots. I staged an equivalent temporary project under `/tmp/hyp-formal.rMtIYN` with the current RTL and ran the unmodified script there.
- Temporary formal run: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Single Shared RV32M Multiplier
motivation:
The current ALU builds three independent wide products every cycle:
signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned, and signed*unsigned. Those products feed
the same EX-stage result mux as ordinary integer operations, so their area and
routing pressure can lower Fmax even when most instructions are ADD/logic,
branches, loads, and stores. CoreMark does use MUL, so making multiplication
multi-cycle risks losing IPC; however all RV32M multiply variants can be
implemented by one signedness-controlled 33x33 product with no latency change.
This is a narrower and lower-risk micro-optimization than the prior failed
DIV/REM interlock attempt because it leaves divider behavior, pipeline stalls,
and RVFI ordering untouched.
hypothesis:
Replace the three unconditional multiplier expressions in rtl/alu.sv with a
single shared 33-bit-by-33-bit product. For MUL, zero-extend both operands and
use the low 32 bits; for MULH, sign-extend both operands; for MULHSU,
sign-extend rs1 and zero-extend rs2; for MULHU, zero-extend both operands.
Select product[63:32] for the high-half operations and product[31:0] for MUL.
Keep the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS substitutions exactly as they are and leave
DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU single-cycle for this experiment. The expected win is lower
LUT/DSP use and less EX-stage mux/routing pressure, improving median Fmax
without changing CoreMark instruction count, branch behavior, memory behavior,
or the single-retire RVFI contract.

changes.description:
Replace mul_ss, mul_uu, and mul_su with one signed 33x33 product whose
operand sign-extension is selected from the ALU multiply opcode. Route the
existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU cases from that shared product, preserve
exact RV32M bit results, keep ALTOPS formulas unchanged, and do not change
DIV/REM implementation or any pipeline control.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- Replaced the three independent multiplier products in `rtl/alu.sv` with one shared multiplier path.
- Added per-operand signedness selection for RV32M multiply operations:
  - `MUL` and `MULHU`: zero-extend both operands.
  - `MULH`: sign-extend both operands.
  - `MULHSU`: sign-extend `rs1` / operand `a`, zero-extend `rs2` / operand `b`.
- The shared product uses signed 33-bit operands and keeps the low 64 product bits; `MUL` selects `[31:0]`, and the high-half multiply operations select `[63:32]`.
- Left the `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` substitution formulas unchanged.
- Left `DIV`, `DIVU`, `REM`, and `REMU` unchanged and single-cycle.

## Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis plan.
- The product signal is stored as 64 bits rather than 66 bits because RV32M only needs product bits `[63:0]`; the 33-bit input extension still preserves the correct signed/unsigned 32x32 result bits.

## Concerns

- No known RTL concerns. The multiplier remains combinational, so latency and pipeline control are unchanged.
- Running `bash formal/run_all.sh` directly in the worktree failed before elaboration because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a protected path and the script could not remove staged `.sv` files there (`Operation not permitted`). I reran the same script from a `/tmp` copy with the symlink dereferenced.

## Verification

- PASS: `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`
- PASS: `bash formal/run_all.sh` from `/tmp/alu-formal-run.hRBUSw` after copying current `rtl/` and dereferencing `formal/riscv-formal`; final tally was `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.
- PASS: `pytest -q test/test_alu.py`
